title: The Turing Test
description: Alan Turing proposes the imitation game, asking whether machines can think.
detail: In his landmark paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence,' Turing proposes that if a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human, it can be considered intelligent — laying the philosophical foundation for all AI research that followed.

title: Birth of AI
description: The Dartmouth Conference coins the term 'Artificial Intelligence'.
detail: John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and others gathered at Dartmouth College, formally defining AI as a field. The ambitious proposal: 'every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it.'

title: ELIZA — The First Chatbot
description: Joseph Weizenbaum creates ELIZA, a program that simulates a psychotherapist.
detail: ELIZA used pattern matching and scripted responses to simulate conversation. Users would become emotionally attached despite knowing it was a program — a phenomenon now called the ELIZA effect — foreshadowing our complex relationships with AI.

title: Expert Systems Peak
description: Rule-based expert systems like XCON bring AI into business and medicine.
detail: XCON (R1) at Digital Equipment Corporation saved millions annually by configuring computer orders. Medical expert systems like MYCIN could diagnose blood infections better than junior doctors. AI briefly becomes big business — until the first 'AI Winter' arrived.

title: Backpropagation
description: Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams make neural network training practical.
detail: The gradient descent backpropagation algorithm finally made it feasible to train multi-layer neural networks. While the idea existed earlier, this publication ignited the first neural network wave. Geoffrey Hinton would later win the Turing Award for this work.

title: Deep Blue Defeats Kasparov
description: IBM's chess computer defeats world champion Garry Kasparov 3.5–2.5.
detail: A watershed moment in AI history — a machine defeating the world's best human at chess. Deep Blue evaluated 200 million positions per second. Kasparov accused IBM of cheating. The match marked the first time a reigning world chess champion lost to a computer under standard tournament conditions.

title: LeNet & Convolutional Networks
description: Yann LeCun demonstrates CNNs for digit recognition at scale.
detail: LeNet-5 achieved near-human performance on the MNIST handwritten digit database, processing 500 checks per second for major US banks. The architecture — convolutional layers, pooling, and fully connected layers — would become the blueprint for all modern computer vision.

title: The Deep Learning Renaissance
description: Hinton's deep belief networks show stacked networks can be effectively trained.
detail: Hinton and Salakhutdinov published 'Reducing the Dimensionality of Data with Neural Networks' in Science, showing that deep networks could be pre-trained layer by layer. This reignited interest in neural networks and ended the second AI Winter.

title: AlexNet — The Deep Learning Revolution
description: AlexNet wins ImageNet by a 10.8% margin, launching a new paradigm.
detail: Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey Hinton's AlexNet achieved 15.3% top-5 error on ImageNet — the runner-up had 26.2%. This wasn't just a win; it was a paradigm rupture. Every major tech company pivoted to deep learning within months.

title: GANs — Machines Learn to Create
description: Ian Goodfellow invents Generative Adversarial Networks at a bar.
detail: Legend has it Goodfellow conceived GANs in a Montreal bar during a debate. Two networks — a generator and discriminator — compete until the generator creates indistinguishable fakes. The paper sparked the entire field of AI-generated art, synthetic data, and deepfakes.

title: AlphaGo — Mastering the Ancient Game
description: DeepMind's AlphaGo defeats Lee Sedol 4–1 at Go, the most complex board game.
detail: Go has more possible positions than atoms in the observable universe. Experts said computers were decades away from mastering it. Move 37 in Game 2 — a move no human would make — has been called the most beautiful move ever played. It came from an alien intelligence.

title: Attention Is All You Need
description: The Transformer architecture is introduced, changing everything.
detail: Eight Google researchers published a paper that would reshape civilization. The self-attention mechanism allowed models to process entire sequences in parallel, capturing long-range dependencies effortlessly. Every major AI system since — GPT, BERT, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion — is built on Transformers.

title: GPT-1 & BERT — The Pre-training Era
description: OpenAI and Google release large language models pretrained on the internet.
detail: GPT introduced autoregressive pre-training. BERT introduced bidirectional pre-training via masked language modeling. Together they proved that large models pre-trained on raw text could be fine-tuned to solve almost any NLP task with minimal labeled data — beginning the foundation model era.

title: GPT-3 — Emergent Intelligence
description: 175 billion parameters unlock capabilities nobody programmed.
detail: GPT-3 could write essays, generate code, compose poetry, and answer questions — abilities that emerged from scale alone, not explicit training. The term 'emergent capabilities' entered the discourse. Few-shot learning meant providing 3 examples was enough to teach it entirely new tasks.

title: DALL-E & CLIP — Vision Meets Language
description: OpenAI connects vision and language, enabling text-to-image generation.
detail: CLIP learned visual concepts from natural language supervision at internet scale. DALL-E could then generate images from text prompts. The combination unlocked a new creative modality. 'An avocado armchair' would become a cultural meme representing AI's surreal imagination.

title: ChatGPT & Stable Diffusion
description: AI reaches 100 million users in 60 days. A cultural revolution begins.
detail: ChatGPT became the fastest-growing consumer application in history. Stable Diffusion was open-sourced, democratizing image generation for everyone. AI stopped being a research topic and became a daily reality for hundreds of millions — artists, developers, students, professionals.

title: The Copilot Era
description: AI-assisted development becomes the new standard for building software.
detail: GitHub Copilot reaches millions of developers. GPT-4 passes bar exams and medical licensing tests. Meta releases Llama openly. Anthropic ships Claude. Google unveils Gemini. AI stopped being one company's advantage and became the bedrock of the entire software industry.

title: Multimodal Agents
description: AI agents can see, reason, code, and act across the digital world.
detail: GPT-4o processes text, images, and audio natively. Computer-using agents browse the web, write and run code, fill forms, and orchestrate multi-step tasks. The loop closed: AI could now perceive, reason, plan, and act — the OODA loop realized in software.

title: Reasoning Models
description: Chain-of-thought reasoning unlocks PhD-level problem solving.
detail: OpenAI's o1, DeepSeek-R1, and Claude 3.7 Sonnet introduced extended thinking — models that reason step by step before answering, dramatically outperforming on math, science, and coding benchmarks. The quality gap between AI and human experts collapsed in specialized domains.

title: You Are Here
description: AI is embedded in every workflow — and it chooses its own aesthetic.
detail: The page you're reading right now has its color palette, typography, and visual style chosen daily by an AI analyzing global design trends. AI no longer just assists — it participates in its own presentation. The boundary between tool and collaborator has dissolved.
